Severity: warning
Detailed Message: This dereference is not guarded by a non-null check, whereas other dereferences are guarded.

Location-start-col: 23
Location-end-line: 292

  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The reported sink at `/lib/http_client.c:292` is the dereference of `req` in `if (req->body_size < req->content_length)`. CodeQL likely flagged it because the same function later contains `if (getenv(\"BITLBEE_DEBUG\") && req)`, which looks like a null-check of `req`. However, along the real program paths, `req` is not nullable when `http_incoming_data()` runs. `http_incoming_data()` is a `static` internal callback, not a public API, and every legitimate caller passes the same allocated `struct http_request *req` object as callback data. The function already dereferences `req` many times before line 292 (`req->inpa`, `req->ssl`, `req->fd`, `req->flags`, etc.), showing the code relies on the invariant that `req` is valid. Therefore the later `&& req` check is merely redundant/defensive debug code, not evidence of a feasible null path. In real deployment, an attacker controlling HTTP replies can influence response contents and connection state, but cannot make BitlBee schedule `http_incoming_data()` with a NULL `req` through the normal callback chain. A crash here would require some separate memory corruption or impossible internal misuse, not this dataflow.",
  "FP_Reason": [
    "A1"
  ],
  "evidence": "Relevant path: `http_dorequest()` allocates `req` with `g_new0(struct http_request, 1)` at line 46 and, if setup fails, frees it and returns NULL at lines 60-62, so no callback is registered with a NULL request. On success, the same `req` is passed as callback data to `proxy_connect(..., http_connected, req)` or `ssl_connect(..., http_ssl_connected, req)` at lines 49 and 54. `http_connected()` then registers `http_incoming_data` via `b_input_add(source, B_EV_IO_READ, http_incoming_data, req)` at line 154. `proxy_connected()` forwards the original callback data unchanged via `func(data, source, B_EV_IO_READ)` at `lib/proxy.c:103`. The SSL layer likewise forwards the original `req` unchanged through `conn->func(conn->data, ...)` in `lib/ssl_openssl.c:138,176,201` and `lib/ssl_gnutls.c:310,351,366`. Inside `http_incoming_data()`, `req` is dereferenced immediately at lines 208, 213/229, 243/246, 256, 266, 273, 277, 286, and only later checked in debug code at line 298. Since the function is `static`, there is no external direct caller that could legally invoke it with `data == NULL`.",
  "new_reason:": ""
}"
}

  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The reported sink at `lib/json.c:365` is a real dereference of `string`, but the null path is not feasible. That write executes only in the second parsing pass while the parser is inside a string (`flag_string` set). On every feasible path that sets `flag_string`, `string` is assigned from storage that has already been allocated and checked. If allocation fails, the code exits through `e_alloc_failure` and returns an error instead of continuing to the write. In real use, attacker-controlled JSON can make the parser enter this branch, but it cannot make `string` be NULL there; low-memory or allocator-failure cases are handled as parse failure, not as a NULL-dereference crash.",
  "FP_Reason": [
    "A1"
  ],
  "evidence": "`string` is initialized to NULL at `lib/json.c:260`, but the sink is reachable only under `if (flags & flag_string)` at `lib/json.c:272`.\nFor JSON string values, the parser first calls `new_value(&state, &top, &root, &alloc, json_string)` and aborts on failure (`lib/json.c:561-562`). Inside `new_value()`, the actual allocation is checked: `if (!(value->u.string.ptr = (json_char *) json_alloc(...))) return 0;` (`lib/json.c:149-153`). Only after that success does the code set `flag_string` and assign `string = top->u.string.ptr` (`lib/json.c:565-567`).\nFor object keys, the object storage is allocated earlier in `new_value(..., json_object)` and checked there: `if (!((*(void **) &value->u.object.values) = json_alloc(...))) return 0;` followed by `value->_reserved.object_mem = ...` (`lib/json.c:135-142`). If that allocation fails, object creation aborts immediately at `lib/json.c:544-545`. Later key parsing only does `string = (json_char *) top->_reserved.object_mem` after the successful object setup (`lib/json.c:673-675`).\n`string` is reset to NULL only when the closing quote ends string mode and `flag_string` is cleared (`lib/json.c:390-396`). Therefore the combination `string == NULL` and execution of `string[string_length++] = ...` at line 365 is not reachable.",
  "new_reason:": ""
}"

Rule Description: Using an uninitialized variable may lead to undefined results.

Detailed Message: Variable 'i' is not initialized.
Location-file: /lib/url.c
Location-start-line: 63
Location-start-col: 22
Location-end-line: 63
Location-end-col: 22",
  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The report is a false positive. The flagged sink is `strncpy(url->file, i, MAX_STRING)` at `lib/url.c:63`, and the alleged problematic value is local pointer `i`. However, `i` is assigned immediately in the controlling condition at `lib/url.c:60`: `if ((i = strchr(s, '/')) == NULL)`. In C, that assignment is executed before the comparison, so `i` is always initialized at that point. More importantly, line 63 is only reachable through the `else` branch, which means `strchr` returned a non-NULL pointer; therefore `i` is not only initialized but also non-NULL on every path to the reported line. There is no feasible runtime path where uninitialized `i` reaches line 63, so this cannot cause the claimed CWE-476-style crash. Real callers such as `lib/http_client.c:542` (parsing a server-supplied redirect URL) and `conf.c:309` (parsing proxy configuration) do reach `url_set`, but attacker/control input does not change the branch invariant: if no `/` exists, execution takes the NULL branch and sets `url->file` to `/`; if line 63 executes, `i` is valid.",
  "FP_Reason": ["D3.Path-Insensitive Analysis"],
  "evidence": "Relevant code in `lib/url.c`:\n`if ((i = strchr(s, '/')) == NULL) {`\n`    strcpy(url->file, \"/\");`\n`} else {`\n`    strncpy(url->file, i, MAX_STRING);`\n`    *i = 0;`\n`}`\nThis proves the only path to line 63 is the branch where `i = strchr(s, '/')` returned non-NULL. So the tool effectively invented an infeasible path where `i` is used without that assignment/guard. Reachable call sites confirm real-world context but not exploitability: `lib/http_client.c:539-545` passes redirect `loc` into `url_set`, and `conf.c:307-313` passes config `ini->value` into `url_set`; in both cases, malformed input does not bypass the `strchr` guard.",
  "new_reason:": ""
}"
}

  "analysis": "The reported source is `type = xt_find_attr(node, \"type\")` in `protocols/jabber/presence.c:30`. That source can legitimately be NULL when a `<presence>` stanza has no `type` attribute; `xt_find_attr()` returns NULL for missing attributes (`lib/xmltree.c:501-532`). The reported sink is `strcmp(type, \"unavailable\")` at `protocols/jabber/presence.c:100`, which would indeed be unsafe if reached with `type == NULL`. However, this sink is guarded by the immediately preceding control flow: `if (type == NULL) { ... } else if (strcmp(type, \"unavailable\") == 0) { ... }`. Because line 100 is the `else if` arm of the same chain, it is reachable only when `type != NULL`, and `type` is not reassigned between the check and the `strcmp` calls. In real XMPP usage, a missing `type` attribute is the normal 'available presence' case and is fully handled in the first branch, so an attacker cannot trigger `strcmp(NULL, ...)` here.   "FP_Reason": ["D3"],
  "evidence": "Relevant code in `protocols/jabber/presence.c`: line 30 `char *type = xt_find_attr(node, \"type\");`, line 48 `if (type == NULL) {`, line 100 `} else if (strcmp(type, \"unavailable\") == 0) {`, followed by more `else if (strcmp(type, ...))` checks. Since these comparisons are in the `else if` chain, they execute only when `type` is non-NULL. `type` is a local variable assigned once and never modified before line 100. Supporting source behavior in `lib/xmltree.c`: `xt_find_attr()` returns NULL when the requested attribute is absent (`if (!node) return NULL; ... return node->attr[i].value;`). Therefore, the only way `type` is NULL is the exact case that is diverted into the first branch, making the reported null-dereference path unreachable at runtime.",
  "new_reason:": ""

However, the report is a false positive because all reachable production and test paths initialize `global.conf` before any call can reach this function. In the real program entry point, `unix.c:86-88` sets `global.conf_file`, calls `global.conf = conf_load(argc, argv)`, and exits immediately if that returns NULL. Only after that does `unix.c:130-146` call `bitlbee_daemon_init()` / `bitlbee_inetd_init()`, which are the paths that later register IPC handlers and may eventually invoke `ipc_to_children_str()`. In the test binary, `tests/check.c:116-117` also initializes `global.conf` before running tests. Therefore the warning depends on ignoring whole-program initialization order and treating `ipc_to_children_str()` as if it were callable before startup initialization. Also, `global` itself is a file-scope object (`unix.c:52`, `tests/check.c:10`) and is zero-initialized by C, so this is not a true 'uninitialized storage' situation. There is a separate nearby null-dereference risk in the REHASH handlers if `conf_load(0, NULL)` fails after `g_free(global.conf)`, but that would crash earlier at `ipc.c:111` or `ipc.c:291`, not at the reported sink.",

Only afterwards, `unix.c:130-146` enters runmode-specific startup and may call `bitlbee_daemon_init()`. The startup path in `bitlbee.c:158-163` calls `ipc_master_load_state()` / `ipc_master_listen_socket()` only after `global.conf` is already loaded. There is no sanitizer or null-check before the indirect call. But `unix.c:77-79` has an early path: if `argv[1]` is `-x`, `main()` does `return crypt_main(argc, argv);` before `log_init()`. In the first pass, `string` may be `0`, but the macro does not dereference it; it only increments `string_length`. In the second pass, every path that enables string parsing first performs allocation through `new_value(...)`. If that allocation fails, `new_value` returns `0` and the caller immediately jumps to `e_alloc_failure`, so execution never reaches the `string_add(...)` write with a null buffer. The reported line `287` is only `string_add('\\f')`. For normal JSON string values, `json_parse_ex()` does `if (!new_value(&state, &top, &root, &alloc, json_string)) goto e_alloc_failure;` and only after that sets `flags |= flag_string; string = top->u.string.ptr;` (`lib/json.c:559-568`). In `new_value()`, the second-pass string buffer allocation is checked: `if (!(value->u.string.ptr = (json_char *) json_alloc(...))) return 0;` (`lib/json.c:147-152`). In real call sites, `json_parse()` is used for network responses, and parse failure is handled as a NULL return; for example `twitter_parse_response()` checks `if ((ret = json_parse(...)) == NULL)` (`protocols/twitter/twitter_lib.c:234-237`), and `json_o_get()` is NULL-safe (`lib/json_util.c:35-40`).",

If setup fails, `http_dorequest()` frees `req` and returns `NULL` before any callback is registered, so no later callback can receive a null `req`. The event layer, proxy layer, and SSL layer all forward the callback `data` pointer unchanged; they do not synthesize `NULL`. Therefore an attacker-controlled HTTP response cannot drive `req` to `NULL`, and this location is not realistically reachable as a null dereference. The likely reason CodeQL reported it is the presence of defensive null checks elsewhere (for example `http_close()` and the debug print), which do not apply to this callback path.",
  "FP_Reason": [
    "A1"
  ],
  "evidence": "`req` is allocated in `http_dorequest()` with `g_new0` and on connection error the function does `http_free(req); return NULL;` before any callback registration (`lib/http_client.c:46-62`). `http_incoming_data()` is `static` (`lib/http_client.c:202`), so external code cannot call it directly. Its only registrations are `b_input_add(source, B_EV_IO_READ, http_incoming_data, req)` at `lib/http_client.c:154` and `req->inpa = b_input_add(req->fd, ..., http_incoming_data, req)` at `lib/http_client.c:266`; the only direct call is the self-recursive `return http_incoming_data(data, source, cond);` at `lib/http_client.c:261-262`, which preserves the same non-null `data`. The event backends pass stored callback data unchanged: GLib uses `closure->data = data` then `closure->function(closure->data, ...)` (`lib/events_glib.c:120-122,92`), and libevent uses `b_ev->data = data` then `b_ev->function(b_ev->data, ...)` (`lib/events_libevent.c:149,183,192`). On the SSL path, OpenSSL stores `conn->data = data` and later invokes `conn->func(conn->data, 0, conn, cond)` (`lib/ssl_openssl.c:82-89,199-201`); the GnuTLS/NSS backends do the same pattern. The null checks that likely triggered the rule are defensive only: `if (getenv(\"BITLBEE_DEBUG\") && req)` in `lib/http_client.c:298` and `if (!req) return;` in public helper `http_close()` at `lib/http_client.c:689-693`. Rule Description: A dereferenced pointer is not checked for nullness in this location, but it is checked in other locations. Dereferencing a null pointer leads to undefined results.

  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "FP. The reported dereference at `lib/http_client.c:209` is inside the private callback `http_incoming_data()` and would only be dangerous if its `data` argument were `NULL`. That is not reachable in this codebase. The sink itself is real (`req->inpa` would crash if `req == NULL`), so this is not a B1 source/sink problem; instead, the report misses the callback-registration context. `http_incoming_data()` is file-local, is only registered internally with a previously allocated `struct http_request *req`, and the event/proxy layers forward that exact pointer unchanged. The only `NULL` check on `req` is in the public helper `http_close()`, which intentionally accepts `NULL` as a convenience API and does not imply that the internal callback must also accept `NULL`. In real use, a remote peer can influence HTTP bytes, but cannot make the internal callback receive a `NULL` request object. This is therefore a false positive caused by shallow interprocedural/context reasoning rather than an exploitable CWE-476 at the reported line.",
  "FP_Reason": ["A1"],
  "evidence": "`http_incoming_data` is a `static` function (`lib/http_client.c:34-36`), so external code cannot call it directly. `req` is allocated in `http_dorequest()` and passed as callback data to connection setup (`lib/http_client.c:46-55`). After the request is sent, `http_connected()` registers the callback as `b_input_add(source, B_EV_IO_READ, http_incoming_data, req)` (`lib/http_client.c:149-155`). The event backends preserve and invoke the same pointer: GLib stores `closure->data = data` and later calls `closure->function(closure->data, ...)` (`lib/events_glib.c:120-121,92`); libevent stores `b_ev->data = data` and later calls `b_ev->function(b_ev->data, ...)` (`lib/events_libevent.c:182-183,149`). The proxy layer also forwards the same pointer via `func(data, source, B_EV_IO_READ)` (`lib/proxy.c:103`). By contrast, the only place that checks `if (!req)` is the public API `http_close()` (`lib/http_client.c:689-693`), which is a different contract from the internal callback path.",
  "new_reason:": ""
}"
}
